section: Abstract
text: Reactors can be constructed with initial enrichments as low as $1.25\%$ U-235; initial conversion ratios of as high as 0.8 can be obtained with enrichment of less than $2\%$ .

section: Abstract
text: Highly enriched uranium would be added as make-up fuel, and such reactors could probably be operated for burnups as high as 60,000 Mwd/ton before buildup of fission products would make replacement of the fuel desirable.

section: Abstract
text: A typical circulating fuel reactor of this class might contain an initial inventory of 3600 tons of $1.8\%$ enriched uranium, operated at 640 Mw (thermal), and generate a net of 260 Mw (electrical).

section: Abstract
text: The total fuel cycle cost would be approximately 1.3 mills/kwhr, of which 1.0 mill is burnup of enriched U-235.

text: The volume of fuel in the core should be evaluated in terms of the external volume for a circulating fuel reactor, which is about 0.56 cu ft per thermal megawatt, or 360 cu ft for a 260 Mw (electrical) plant.

section: SURVEY OF LOW ENRICHMENT MOLTEN-SALT REACTORS
text: It is evident that one must pay for conversion ratios above 0.8 with enrichments of over $2\%$ .

text: The latter problem was looked at briefly, using as a basis the calculations of Blomeke and Todd (ORNL-2127), and assuming a buildup of Pu sub infinity as defined in the Brookhaven Geneva Paper 461.

section: SURVEY OF LOW ENRICHMENT MOLTEN-SALT REACTORS
text: However, it might be possible to operate a reactor such as Case 8 of Table II for as

section: SURVEY OF LOW ENRICHMENT MOLTEN-SALT REACTORS
text: long as 30 years at $640\mathrm{Mw}$ (thermal) without a need for the U-235 inventory to increase by more than a factor of two, and with a breeding ratio averaging greater than $50\%$ , without any reprocessing.

section: SURVEY OF LOW ENRICHMENT MOLTEN-SALT REACTORS
text: For purposes of estimating the fuel cycle cost, a life of 10 years without reprocessing was assumed.

section: SURVEY OF LOW ENRICHMENT MOLTEN-SALT REACTORS
text: For this period, the U-235 concentration would probably not have to be increased over its initial value, and the breeding ratio should average at least $60\%$ .

section: SURVEY OF LOW ENRICHMENT MOLTEN-SALT REACTORS
text: Applying the formula of ORNL-2500, but using the thermal cycle of the reference design molten-salt reactor (CF-58-5-3), the chemical reprocessing charges and fuel inventory charges are 0.034 mill/kwhr and 0.14 mill/kwhr, respectively.

section: SURVEY OF LOW ENRICHMENT MOLTEN-SALT REACTORS
text: Ten-year depreciation and capital charges on the base salt amount to 0.093 mill/kwhr.

section: SURVEY OF LOW ENRICHMENT MOLTEN-SALT REACTORS
text: Burnup of U-235 would be $\sim$ 1.0 mill/kwhr at a conversion ratio of 0.6.

section: SURVEY OF LOW ENRICHMENT MOLTEN-SALT REACTORS
text: Thus, total fuel cycle costs would be approximately 1.3 mills/kwhr.

section: SURVEY OF LOW ENRICHMENT MOLTEN-SALT REACTORS
text: This reactor was based on Case 8 of Table II, using a total fuel volume of 600 ft³ and an inventory of 36 tons of uranium of 1.8% enrichment.

section: SURVEY OF LOW ENRICHMENT MOLTEN-SALT REACTORS
text: The ten-year reprocessing cycle represents a fuel life of approximately 60,000 Mwd/ton.
